docs.text: Hello, in this site I will try to post as many of my writings as I can.
docs.title: Home

docs.text: I have been reviewing the State of AI 2023 , and inspired by it, I wanted to share some of my thoughts. There is no question that the use of text, images, and sound, also called "multi-modality" in Large Language Models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT, is the topic of the day. However, as someone working with 3D perception sensors, I believe the true revolution will happen when Machine Learning incorporates spatial thinking. We already have excellent 3D modeling in games and simulations. Still, the actual floodgate opening will come with fleets of vehicles adding 3D sensors such as LiDARs. With massive datasets collected on the roads, there will be an industry similar to image object recognition, which started in 2012. There will be a need to take the point cloud data produced by LiDARS and abstract it into a mesh of polygons similar to that used in games. The shapes of various objects, even if partially obstructed, will need to be classified into thousands of categories even when rotated at any angle. Abstraction might be the key. Recognizing primitives, such as wheels, doors, and windows, might give us a graph of relationships indicating the closest relationship to an automobile, adding a horse to a mix might give us a buggy, and so on. References: State of AI 2023 PDF
docs.title: AI 3D

docs.text: State of AI 2023 PDF
docs.title: References:

docs.text: Your career roadmap. Manage the Distractions In today's world, distractions are abound. Reader beware! As you are reading it, I am distracting you from what you really should be doing. Every day, there are hundreds of new social media posts, new great ideas, and gadgets, which, we feel, we should become familiar with. There is a whole advisory industry, especially on YouTube, making millions by telling us what to think and what to buy. You could refuse to deal with it and close the doors, many have deleted their Facebook, or Instagram. In fact if you do so, there are many retro-niche markets out there you could be doing. Or, you can try to manage distractions wisely. Personally, I love learning new ideas, if I had an unlimited amount of time I would study more things, but that is not the reality and I do have to make selections. Please read on, I have some techniques to follow below. We all are facing dilemmas In addition to tiny distractions, here are few dilemmas I am constantly facing: Should I invest the time and effort and get an advanced degree, or at least a new certification? Should I be studying the machine learning, the autonomous driving, or the robotics? Should I spend time to write, or update my own apps? Should I work on weekends with friends on a new start-up? Trying to do everything will surely lead to a failure and depression, so I have to be very diligent at prioritizing. Make a list, make some hard choices. Eliminate bad choices It is important to eliminate things you do not want to focus on: "Commodity jobs. " These days everyone, and their grandma, can do IT. Yes, pun intended, so please focus and be original. " Nay sayers " - Nothing will bring you down as a "friend" who is a psychopathic pessimist, most of the people are. Do not talk to them . " Complainers " - Again, people with victim syndrome will justify about anything in their life, then spend a rest of the day on Facebook. Do not talk to them . " False authority " - Surprise, your bosses, parents, what-have-you, could be making some terrible career decisions. Ask me how do I know. " One word: plastics " might have been a good advice in sixties, sillicon in fifties, Pets.com in 2000 , and "There is an app for this" in 2010, but today, maybe you should follow Mike Rowe . I cannot list all the bad things you have to eliminate, they will be specific to your situation, but I suggest you list then in your little red book. Choose what is right for you So, you'd ask yourself the question, what is the next, next thing? What should I focus on in the next few months? Review your interests, talents, experience, and build upon it. If you have nothing goinf on yet, follow what interests you because otherwise, you probably won't stay on course. Do not jump around, select one single thing you want to do in 2 years. Life is longer than you think, you can change your mind 20 times in your career. Once the decision about your future is made, you can relax and study. Pivots I do make this kind of career direction choices every-so-often, then I micro-pivot by a few degrees. I believe we should always build upon our prior experiences. Starting from scratch is generally a terrible idea. Here are some of my own pivots : in 1997 I decided to leave USMC and study computers in 1998 I changed from Electronics/Automotive Mechanics to Web IT in 2001 I changed to Java Web programming in 2009 I changed to Android Java Mobile programming in 2016 I started to study machine learning programming for automotive in 2017 I started to work for Harman on Android Infotament Systems in 2019 I started to work in ADAS, Autonomous Driving Checkpoints to follow Here is the path I have developed and follow in my career, you can use then as checkpoints: Pick something you want to do about 2 years from now, it has to be meaningful to you it has to be useful -- people must be willing to pay for it. Study it. Get basic understanding of what you are doing. Beginnings are slow and hard. Study a lot, at some point you will see a dramatic increase in your understanding, look up " David Goggins " if you wonder how much you should practice. Listen to Malcolm Gladwell’s book "Outliers" to understand how much you need to study. Read everything written on the subject. Carry a few "want to read" articles (eBooks) with you at all times. There is more articles published then anyone can consume. I have a Kindle with over 900 scientific papers on it, most unread. Become a "go-to expert", teach at meetups, organize a special interest group, Write articles, or blog post, they do not have to be very good you are doing it for yourself. Write books, it is a tall order, I know. Make turorials (e.g. videos on YouTube). Get references for prior-jobs well done. Review job requirements, update LinkedIn profile accordingly. Find professional connections on LinkedIn, more is better, I have about 8,000. Apply for the jobs. Prepare for the interviews. Adjust your path and pivot every two years. Nerd chart to follow Being a manager, and a nerd really, I decided to make a flow chart of my improvement process. Read it, following the arrows, do it several times. 15-minute rule The great opportunities come to people who are highly trained and ready. There is a saying "When the student is ready, the teacher appears." You have to be mindful to remind yourself every 15 minutes to make a decision on " what to do now" to become a better human being . This is one of my critical principles, this 15-minute evaluation of the time well spent. Sometimes the answer will be to eat, or sleep, or laugh with friends, sometimes it will be to get out to breathe and exercise. The differentiator, however, will be how often you will decide to study and work hard on your goals. It is all about good habits . Once again, in case you have missed it, your habbits will define your sucess . Suprisingly, a simple statement works for me very well, "I shall not be a lazy person." That is all that is needed, I pick up and get going. Back to Basics The next aspect of the career development is keeping up to date with the basics , in my case, the computer science fundamentals. The next section talks about computer science, but it applies to anything, really. The military professionals will tell you "Smooth is fast and fast is smooth". Honestly, it does not matter how hard you work on daily basis, you will not pass the "whiteboard hazing", err "the interview", without any preparation. I lost countless of interviews with fine companies like Amazon, Google and Facebook to what I've felt was a "power trip" of some engineer without any social skills in a cookie factory. For years I was saying, "Why do I need that? I make good money here and now". Only later, I have read books and articles on interviewing and realized that the "whiteboard" is simply a thing the computer companies do and that people prepare by studying algorithms and zen-koan-like mind-bender problems for months . This is some great news for idle students, but not so great for busy people who have real jobs (and families). This is a particularly painful for managers like me who deal with projects and teams and do not write code for a living. Still, to get a job in a top company you have to have outstanding skills . Thankfully, there is a huge backlash, and HR departments are realizing that ability to " invert a binary tree " on a whiteboard has no correlation with creating a beautiful, or useful products. The focus in next few years should be on "thinking on your feet" and diversity of experience . My suggestion for anyone, would be to have a profession-oriented blog and for any software engineer a GitHub account , and to be prolific on daily basis . Keep the chin up Finally, if you are interviewing, do not get discouraged by numerous rejections. In fact, you do not want a job that does not fit you. Eventually, if you really apply yourself, and if you are a half-decent human being, you will find a great team that will love you, while willing to pay well for the privilege . This post should not be considered a "shortcut", but rather, a best practice on how to organize your career raodmap and prioritize your options. Thank you! If you enjoyed it, please share and follow me on Twitter @UkiDLucas and LinkedIn . Disclaimer: I am NOT an Amazon affiliate, I provide links to books, because I want you to read them. Recommended citation: Uki D. Lucas. (2017). "Your career roadmap." LinkedIn.com . https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/how-stay-relevant-your-high-tech-career-uki-dominque-lucas/
docs.title: Your Career Roadmap

docs.text: Quanta Qualia AI QuantaQualiaAI is a project pioneering a new frontier in quantum computing and communication exploring entangled particles such as "traditional" electrons, ions, photons or even neutrinos. Understanding Quantum Mechanics and Quantum Computing The field of Quantum mechanics and computing are very poorly understood even in the science community. Below, I am attempting to provide a set of short questions and answers that hopefully will provide enough background for the conversation. Q: How is it possible for particles to instantly interact over vast distances? We humans have an " illusion " of time dimension and the illusion of space . Let me throw the gravity to the mix, too. In reality, there is a multi-dimensional matrix of vibrations that underpin what we perceive as space-time or the matter (in another words, the things). The time is perceived only in context of events that we "perceive". This matrix can be stable , or it can bend , creating gravity holes , in extreme, but common cases, it can loop on itself. Objects can be entangled by sharing properties of this matrix and in some cases the distance and time can be non-consequential , in which case see perceive the two particles as behaving the same even when separated by light-years of travel. Q: What role does measurement play in entanglement? A: Measurement plays a crucial role in entanglement. When you measure one particle of an entangled pair (or set), the outcome determines the state of the other particle instantaneously. However, until the measurement is made, the particles exist in a superposition of states, and the outcome is fundamentally probabilistic. Q: How do we ensure validity of the probabilistic set of entangled particles? If we were to consider only a pair of entangled particles, the probability of a message would be low. The stability of the quantum particles is low. By using a large set of entangled particles we decrease the error and increase the certainty of the message. Q: How are different types of neutrinos distinguished? A: Neutrinos are distinguished by their flavor: electron neutrinos, muon neutrinos, and tau neutrinos . These flavors are associated with the corresponding charged leptons (electron, muon, tau). Neutrino flavor is identified through the detection of the specific charged lepton produced in a neutrino interaction.
docs.title: Quant Qualia

docs.text: Volunteer Work My work at Harman/Samsung allows me to take some time off work to VOLUNTEER . I would like to spend that time (or much more) advising a not-for-profit organization, or a start-up with a worthy cause. The value I can bring include but are not limited to: Machine-Learning (large data set, image, PCD processing, classification, etc.) IoT (developing distributed and connected measurement electronics) Cloud Solutions (geo-data, microservices connected to Web, large data, apps) Mobile app design any digital System Design Speak, or teach a class and more. My interests are wide-ranging, but I am very interested in: Biology (marine, genetics, evolutionary, ecosystem, conservation, population) Linguistics (migration, population) Robotics (personal, autonomy, smart-cities) Agriculture/Arboriculture (farming and nature preservation) Please contact me on LinkedIn with suggestions and questions.
docs.title: Volunteer Work

docs.text: I decided to write this open letter after an extensive set of interviews I have conducted in recent months, ranging from software interns, through developers, a principal architect, and even upper management. In these interviews, I realized what I already knew but was suppressed because of the COVID-19 pandemic , moving to different states, and being busy at work. The realization is that we all need mentorship . You needed mentorship and apprenticeships in the old days because the information was unavailable. Today, with easy access to libraries, the Internet, and ChatGPT, you can have an AI mentor in any subject, whether for a foreign language, leadership, philosophy, or technical skill. So why did the idea come back to me? We lost something; we spend more time alone , browse the Internet, and get into its rabbit holes . We end up in echo chambers without the objective voice of a friendly mentor. I was thinking how many years, if not decades, I lost because I had no one to guide me. At the same time, I started to think about creating a social club or a meetup for like-minded technology professionals. I am fortunate to live in the Pacific Northwest in the heart of a technology hub. You might ask who I am to think of a new group instead of joining an existing one. First, I did not find what I wanted. Second, I have a very long history of organizing people. When I was younger, in Chicago, for over ten years, I have been running the Google Technology Group with many thousands of members and over 110 conferences and meetups organized, many of them having over 120 participants. It was a great time in the pre-pandemic era. As far as the goals of the group, I listed the following: mentorship friendly but intellectual conversations technical and scientific idea exchange personal development , especially in areas not often discussed, such as habits and virtues like humanity , persistence , being humble , prudence , and many others. I also wanted to ensure that the group is inclusive without any bias regarding race , religion , gender , or political outlook. There was also a question of narrowing the focus and purpose of the group. I am hoping to create this organization for a particular type of people, namely technology architects and scientists . I know the term "technology architects" is an tautology, but this is how I can make it understandable.
